mnemonics: P.U.P.P.E.T. - Prioritize User Processes, Preempt Everything Timely
keyPoints: Thread scheduling is the mechanism by which an OS decides which thread runs on a CPU core and for how long.
Schedulers use algorithms like Round Robin, Priority Scheduling, or Multilevel Feedback Queues to manage thread execution.
The goal is to maximize CPU utilization and system throughput while minimizing waiting time and response time for threads.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'I/O-bound' threads (which wait often) with 'CPU-bound' threads (which use the CPU heavily), leading to poor scheduling choices.
Assuming a preemptive scheduler always gives every thread a fair turn, when priority levels can allow high-priority threads to 'starve' lower-priority ones.
